Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 24 of about 50 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the black/white shape more clearly. It's at cols 48-62 (width 15), rows 18-29 (height 12). The structure:
- Top bar: rows 18-20, cols 48-56 (9 cells)
- Vertical bar: rows 21-26, cols 54-56 (3 cells)
- Bottom bar: rows 27-29, cols 48-56 (9 cells)
Wait, let me re-examine:
Row 18: BBBBBBBBB (cols 48-56)
Row 19: BWBBWBBWB (cols 48-56) - has W (white) dots
Row 20: BBBBBBBBB (cols 48-56)
Row 21: BBB (cols 54-56)
Row 22: BWB (cols 54-56) - has W dot
Row 23: BBB (cols 54-56)
Row 24: BBB (cols 54-56)
Row 25: BWB (cols 54-56) - has W dot
Row 26: BBB (cols 54-56)
Row 27: BBBBBBB (cols 50-56)
Row 28: BWBBWBB (cols 50-56) - has W dots
Row 29: BBBBBBB (cols 50-56)
So the black/white shape has:
- Top bar: rows 18-20, cols 48-56 (9 cols)
- Vertical bar: rows 21-26, cols 54-56 (3 cols) - on the RIGHT side of the top bar
- Bottom bar: rows 27-29, cols 50-56 (7 cols) - extending LEFT from the vertical bar
Wait, the bottom bar is cols 50-56 (7 cols), not 9 cols. Let me recheck.
Row 27: `bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBB` - B starts at position... let me count from the substring.
The substring is `lines[i][38:61]`, so position 0 in the substring corresponds to col 38.
Row 27: `bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBB`
b(16)B(7) = 23 chars, so B starts at col 38+16=54 and ends at col 38+16+7-1=60.
Hmm, that's cols 54-60. But the segmentation says the B shape is at cols 48-62. Let me re-examine.
Actually, the segmentation says the B shape is at cols 48-62. Let me check row 18 more carefully.
Row 18: `bbbbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBbbbb`
b(12)B(9)b(5) = 26 chars. So B starts at col 38+12=50 and ends at col 38+12+9-1=57.
But the segmentation says cols 48-62. There's a discrepancy. Let me re-examine the segmentation.
Actually, the segmentation says `col 48-62` for the B shape. But my ascii parsing shows the B shape at cols 50-57 (for the top bar). This is confusing.
Wait, I think the issue is that the B shape includes more than just the visible B characters. The segmentation might be including the W (white) dots inside the B shape as part of the same connected component.
Actually, looking at the ascii, the W dots are inside the B shape. The B shape is a connected component that includes both B and W cells? No, the segmentation separates by color, so W cells would be a separate node.
Let me re-examine. The segmentation says the B shape has 64 pixels. Let me count the B cells in the ascii:
Row 18: 9 B cells
Row 19: 9 B cells (with W dots interspersed)
Row 20: 9 B cells
Row 21: 3 B cells
Row 22: 3 B cells (with W dot)
Row 23: 3 B cells
Row 24: 3 B cells
Row 25: 3 B cells (with W dot)
Row 26: 3 B cells
Row 27: 7 B cells
Row 28: 7 B cells (with W dots)
Row 29: 7 B cells
Total: 9+9+9+3+3+3+3+3+3+7+7+7 = 69... but the segmentation says 64 pixels. Hmm.
Actually, I think the W dots are separate nodes. Let me count only the B cells:
Row 18: BBBBBBBBB = 9
Row 19: BWBBWBBWB = 7 B cells (2 W cells)
Row 20: BBBBBBBBB = 9
Row 21: BBB = 3
Row 22: BWB = 2 B cells (1 W cell)
Row 23: BBB = 3
Row 24: BBB = 3
Row 25: BWB = 2 B cells (1 W cell)
Row 26: BBB = 3
Row 27: BBBBBBB = 7
Row 28: BWBBWBB = 5 B cells (2 W cells)
Row 29: BBBBBBB = 7
Total B cells: 9+7+9+3+2+3+3+2+3+7+5+7 = 60... still not 64.
I think my ascii parsing is off. Let me just trust the segmentation for now.
The key observation is that the black/white shape has a different orientation from the gray and yellow shapes:
- Gray shape: vertical bar on the LEFT side of the top bar
- Yellow shape: vertical bar on the LEFT side of the top bar
- Black/white shape: vertical bar on the RIGHT side of the top bar
So the black/white shape is a mirror image of the gray/yellow shapes.
Maybe the goal is to transform the gray shape to match the black/white shape's orientation and position?
Or maybe the goal is to match the gray shape with the yellow shape, and the black/white shape is just a distractor or a reference for the mirror operation?
Let me try a different approach. Let me use ACTION7 or SPACE to see if they change the gray shape's orientation.
Actually, I recall that ACTION7 at col 6-20 moved the gray shape to col 12-20 (right by 6). And at col 12-20, the shape's orientation changed (the vertical bar moved from the left side to the right side).
Wait, let me re-examine. At col 6-20, the gray shape had:
- Top bar: rows 18-20, cols 12-20
- Vertical bar: rows 21-26, cols 12-14
- Bottom bar: rows 27-29, cols 6-14
And at col 12-20 (after ACTION7), the gray shape had:
- Top bar: rows 18-20, cols 12-20
- Vertical bar: rows 21-26, cols 12-14
- Bottom bar: rows 27-29, cols 6-14
Wait, these are the same! The hash is the same, so the shape is the same. I was confused earlier.
Let me re-examine the history more carefully to understand the shape's orientation at different positions.
Actually, I think the shape's orientation is fixed - it's always the same L-shape with the vertical bar on the LEFT side. The hash is the same at all positions, confirming this.
So the goal must be to position the gray shape to match the yellow shape's position. The target is cols 3-17.
But I can't reach col 3-17 from col 6-20 using RIGHT (no impact). And LEFT moves right, not left.
Let me try ACTION7 at col 6-20 to see if it moves the shape to a different position.
